---
title: "Baseline experiment: resting state and the Dolan agonist transient"
subtitle: "Platelet whole-cell model — demo experiment 1 of 4"
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: 2026-06-24
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry: "margin=2.5cm"
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "STIX Two Text"
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
---

## Purpose

This is the **baseline** run — the unperturbed reference that the perturbation
experiments are compared against. It does two things:

1. Shows that the model holds a **stable resting state**, and
2. reproduces an **agonist-evoked Ca²⁺ transient** that *appears similar to* that
   of Dolan & Diamond (*Biophys. J.* 2014) when a physiological agonist mixture
   is applied after a 300 s settling period, and shows that the activated state is
   **maintained** through a further 300 s of stimulation (out to 600 s).

The perturbation experiments reuse this exact set-up and change one model setting
at a time, reading the difference against the reference values established here.

## Model set-up

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| Cell | single platelet, +extracellular Ca²⁺ (1.2 mM) |
| Agonists | thrombin 1 nM, ADP 10 µM, ATP 10 µM (the Dolan standard) |
| Agonist onset | t = 300 s (a 300 s settle precedes stimulation) |
| Duration | 600 s (300 s settle + 300 s stimulation) |
| Model settings | `agonist_delay_s = 300`; all other parameters, counts and rate constants left at their defaults |

The agonists enter as transient time-courses (a rise and a slow decay), not step
inputs — this matches the shape of an agonist bolus reaching a platelet, though
the precise time-course is a modelling choice, not a measurement. The exact
commands to reproduce this run and its figures are in the [Appendix](#appendix).

## Resting state

Started from the Dolan initial conditions (cytosolic Ca²⁺ = 100 nM, dense
tubular system (DTS) store = 250 µM), the model does not sit still, the
 initial values are not equilibrium, so it undergoes a damped
settling transient — an overshoot to ~129 nM by t ≈ 19 s — before converging to
its resting fixed point of **~96 nM cytosolic / ~256 µM store** by ~200 s
(@fig-resting). Resting IP₃ holds at ~47 nM throughout.

![**Resting state with no agonist (600 s).** Left: cytosolic Ca²⁺ relaxes from the imposed initial value (100 nM) through a damped overshoot (~129 nM at ~19 s) to a maintained fixed point of ~96 nM. Centre: the DTS store mirrors this (250 → ~198 → ~256 µM); store-operated entry stays near its low resting level. Right: IP₃ holds at ~47 nM. The model converges to and then holds a resting steady state close to the Dolan resting targets (100 nM / 250 µM); the settling takes a couple of hundred seconds because the imposed initial values are not the model's exact equilibrium.](../figures/demos/resting_calcium.png){#fig-resting width=100%}

The agonist runs below apply their stimulus only
after a **full 300 s settle**, by which point the resting transient is complete
and the trace is flat (@fig-resting). This cleanly separates the start-up settle
from the agonist response — the resting baseline in @fig-agonist is flat right up
to agonist onset at 300 s.

## Agonist response

Applying the agonist mixture at t = 300 s evokes the canonical response
(@fig-agonist): cytosolic Ca²⁺ rises to a sustained **plateau of ~530 nM**
(peak ~530 s), held near ~500 nM out to 600 s, the DTS store empties
(~255 → ~1 µM), store-operated entry switches on, and IP₃ rises to ~220 nM.
Downstream, the αIIbβ3 integrin reaches a PAC-1⁺ active fraction of ~0.87 and
thromboxane A₂ rises to ~1.2 µM.

![**Resting state then Dolan agonist transient (agonist at 300 s, 600 s total).** Left: cytosolic Ca²⁺ — the 300 s resting settle (flat by ~200 s) is followed by a rise to a sustained ~530 nM plateau (peak ~530 s), held near ~500 nM to 600 s. Centre: the DTS store briefly *over-fills* at agonist onset (~255 → ~274 µM at t ≈ 301 s; explained below) and then empties (→ ~1 µM) as the IP₃ receptors open, while store-operated Ca²⁺ entry (right axis, dashed) switches on to sustain the cytosolic plateau. Right: endogenously generated IP₃ rises to ~220 nM. The sustained (rather than transient-then-recovering) plateau is the model's autocrine "second wave" under a saturating agonist; it is store-limited, so the peak height is set by buffering and SOCE rather than by the exact agonist dose.](../figures/demos/baseline_calcium.png){#fig-agonist width=100%}

### The store transiently rises at agonist onset

The brief upward spike in the DTS store at ~300 s (centre panel of @fig-agonist)
is worth noting. It is a **multi-timescale** effect. At onset the fast,
ATP-gated **P2X1** channel admits Ca²⁺ almost immediately and cytosolic Ca²⁺ jumps
from ~96 to ~150 nM within a second — while **IP₃ is still low** (~47 → ~55 nM)
because the slower Gq → PLCβ → IP₃ cascade has not yet built up. SERCA pumps the
Ca²⁺ influx into the store, so the store briefly **over-fills**
(~255 → ~274 µM) before it is drained. A few seconds later IP₃ crosses threshold
(~60–70 nM), the IP₃ receptors open, and the store discharges to ~1 µM. The
spike is therefore the model actually working, rather than an artefact.

## Comparison with real platelets

- **Resting Ca²⁺.** Real resting platelets sit at ~50–100 nM cytosolic Ca²⁺ with
  a high DTS store; the model's resting fixed point (~96 nM / ~256 µM) is within
  this range and close to the Dolan targets. The damped settling transient is a
  consequence of the chosen initial values.
- **Agonist transient.** Real platelets respond to thrombin/ADP with a rapid
  cytosolic Ca²⁺ rise to several hundred nM, sustained in the presence of
  extracellular Ca²⁺ by store-operated entry. This is qualitatively what the model
  shows, and the basis of its validation against Dolan & Diamond (2014) Fig. 4
  (the "5/5" peak / SOCE-differential criteria).
- **Differences.** (i) The model's store empties almost completely
  (~1 µM), whereas Dolan infer a substantial residual. 
  (ii) The real transient is sharper and shows more recovery; 
  the model's sustained plateau reflects its autocrine
  amplification loops under a saturating agonist. (iii) Cytosolic free Ca²⁺ is
  **store-limited** in the model — the peak is nearly insensitive to most single
  parameters; this is the key caveat for interpreting any perturbation of this
  baseline.

## Provenance

The simulations and figures were produced by the demo-experiment driver
`runscripts/manual/runDemoExperiments.py`, which renders the `demo_calcium`
figure module (`models/platelet/analysis/single/demo_calcium.py`, reading the
`CalciumTrace` listener). Both runs used **seed 0** for **600 s** (a 300 s settle
then 300 s of stimulation) on Python 3.11.5, at git commit **`4fe847b9`**. The
exact commit is also stamped in the
footnote of each figure and in the run's `metadata/metadata.json`, so a figure
remains traceable to the code that produced it even when separated from this
document.

## Take-away

The model holds a physiologically reasonable resting state and reproduces an
agonist transient and maintained activated plateau that appear similar to Dolan
& Diamond (2014). It serves as the unperturbed reference: the perturbation
experiments change individual model settings and read the difference against the
values reported here.

## Appendix — reproduce {#appendix}

All four demo experiments are driven by one script
(`runscripts/manual/runDemoExperiments.py`), the single source of truth for the
run conditions: every run settles for 300 s with all agonists at REST, then the
agonists switch on and the run continues for a further 300 s (`length = 600 s`).
From the repository root (`PYTHONPATH=$PWD`):

```bash
# Regenerate every demo-experiment figure (settle 300 s, then 300 s of agonist):
python runscripts/manual/runDemoExperiments.py

# …or just this experiment's two calcium figures:
python runscripts/manual/runDemoExperiments.py \
    --only resting_calcium baseline_calcium
```

The driver declares each run's `RunConfig` (fields map onto
`reconstruction/platelet/run_config.py`; omitted agonist peaks fall back to the
Dolan standard — thrombin 1 nM, ADP 10 µM, ATP 10 µM), runs the simulations, and
copies the figures into `reports/figures/demos/`. The figure module
`models/platelet/analysis/single/demo_calcium.py` (shared by all demo-experiment
calcium figures) plots the `CalciumTrace` columns `ca_cyt_nM`, `ca_dts_uM`,
`soce_flux_nMs` and `ip3_nM`.
